narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've poured into your undergrad studies and LSAT prep, and I want to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to the bar exam as a bit too risky—54.9% chance of not passing, which tips the scales against admission right now. Picture it like this: the model looks at patterns from thousands of students who've walked this road before, and for you, your Year 3 school decile of 6 stood out as the biggest red flag, placing you solidly in the middle of the pack academically when it mattered most toward the end of law school. That was amplified by your Year 1 decile of 4, suggesting your grades started okay but didn't climb as high as those who typically power through to bar success. Your LSAT score of 32.5 pulled in the same concerning direction, not quite hitting the mark that signals strong legal reasoning under pressure. Even your upper-middle family income, while a solid foundation, didn't offset the trend—it's part of a profile where resources haven't always translated to top-tier outcomes here. Your white racial background and choice to go full-time nudged things further toward caution, aligning with past students who've faced similar hurdles. Your undergrad GPA of 2.9 added to that cautious picture, showing a foundation that the model sees as needing more lift. The one bright spot was your gender as female, which has historically leaned toward passing in these patterns, but it wasn't quite enough to counterbalance the rest. It's like the model painted a portrait of someone with potential who's hovered in the middle ranks rather than surging ahead, and law schools need that extra conviction for the bar's high stakes. This isn't a judgment on your worth or drive—it's just the story these numbers told. If you boost those deciles or LSAT in a future round, that picture could change dramatically. Hang in there; you've got time to rewrite this chapter.
